title: Spinal Injuries
description: Spinal cord and vertebral injuries are among the most serious consequences of accidents — potentially causing partial or complete paralysis, chronic pain, and lifelong disability. Whether caused by a car crash, fall, or workplace incident, the steps you take immediately after a spinal injury can have lasting consequences for your health and your legal options.
metaTitle: Spinal Injury Accident Guide — Steps, Evidence & Legal Options
metaDescription: Suffered a spinal injury in an accident? Get guidance on immediate steps, evidence to collect, insurance issues, and when to speak with an attorney.

commonCauses.title: Motor Vehicle Accidents
commonCauses.description: Car, truck, and motorcycle crashes account for nearly half of all spinal cord injuries — the violent force of impact compresses, fractures, or dislocates vertebrae.

commonCauses.title: Slip and Fall Accidents
commonCauses.description: Falls from heights or onto hard surfaces are a leading cause of vertebral fractures, especially in older adults and construction workers.

commonCauses.title: Construction and Workplace Accidents
commonCauses.description: Falls from scaffolding, ladders, or rooftops — and being struck by heavy equipment — frequently result in serious spinal trauma.

commonCauses.title: Diving and Swimming Accidents
commonCauses.description: Diving into shallow water or striking the bottom of a pool can cause catastrophic cervical spine injuries in seconds.

commonCauses.title: Sports and Recreational Injuries
commonCauses.description: Contact sports, ATV accidents, and high-impact recreational activities carry significant risk of vertebral injury.

commonCauses.title: Acts of Violence
commonCauses.description: Gunshot wounds and physical assaults are a cause of spinal cord injury and may support both criminal and civil legal action.

immediateSteps.title: Do not move the injured person — call 911
immediateSteps.description: Suspected spinal injuries require immediate immobilization. Moving someone with a spinal injury before trained responders arrive can cause or worsen paralysis. Call 911 and keep the person still.

immediateSteps.title: Do not remove helmets or restraints
immediateSteps.description: If the person is wearing a helmet or is in a vehicle, do not attempt to remove them without professional guidance. Improper movement can cause permanent cord damage.

immediateSteps.title: Ensure a medical record is created at the scene
immediateSteps.description: Let paramedics evaluate and document the injury on-site. Their report links the incident to the injury and becomes part of the medical record.

immediateSteps.title: Document the scene if you are physically able
immediateSteps.description: If you or a bystander can safely do so, photograph the accident location, contributing hazards, vehicles, and any conditions that caused the injury.

immediateSteps.title: Collect witness information
immediateSteps.description: Witnesses who saw the incident can provide independent accounts of how the injury occurred. Get names and contact information before anyone leaves the scene.

immediateSteps.title: Request copies of all emergency medical records
immediateSteps.description: Emergency room records, paramedic reports, and imaging results from the day of the incident are foundational documents in any spinal injury claim.

immediateSteps.title: Follow up with a spinal specialist immediately
immediateSteps.description: ER care stabilizes the injury. You will need evaluation by a neurosurgeon or orthopedic spine specialist to fully assess the extent of the damage.

immediateSteps.title: Begin documenting functional limitations
immediateSteps.description: Keep a daily record of pain levels, mobility limitations, tasks you cannot perform, and assistance you require. This supports future claims for lost wages, care costs, and quality of life.

items: Paramedic and ambulance reports from day of incident
Emergency room records and physician notes
MRI and CT scan reports of spine and surrounding structures
X-ray results showing vertebral alignment or fractures
Neurological assessment findings
Surgical records if spinal surgery was performed
Photos of the accident location and contributing hazards
Photos of the vehicle, equipment, or surface involved
Police or incident report number
Names and contact information of all witnesses
Surveillance or dashcam footage if available
All spinal specialist, neurosurgeon, and orthopedic surgeon notes
Physical therapy and occupational therapy records
Pain management specialist records
Rehabilitation facility records
Adaptive equipment prescriptions and costs
Long-term care and life care plan from treating physicians
Employer documentation of missed work and inability to return
Pay stubs and earnings records for wage loss calculation
In-home care costs and caregiver invoices
Home modification costs (ramps, wheelchair access)
Vehicle modification costs
Lost future earning capacity documentation
All insurance policies (health, auto, workers' comp, disability)
All written correspondence with insurance adjusters
Any settlement offers received in writing
Workers' compensation claim documentation if workplace-related

timelineRisks.action: Do not move an injured person. Call 911 and keep them still until trained responders arrive. This is a life-safety issue, not just a legal one.
Spinal cord injuries can worsen in the hours after trauma as swelling increases. Ensure the injured person is monitored in a medical facility with spinal injury expertise.
Notify all relevant insurers promptly. For workplace injuries, workers' compensation reporting deadlines may be as short as 30 days.
Secure scene photographs, surveillance footage, and witness statements. Physical evidence at the scene will be altered or removed quickly.
If a government agency, vehicle, or property was involved, California requires a government tort claim to be filed within 6 months. Missing this deadline typically bars the claim.

timelineRisks.risk: Statute of limitations for personal injury
timelineRisks.action: Both states have a 2-year statute of limitations for most personal injury claims. Spinal injury cases require extensive preparation — do not wait to consult an attorney.

insuranceIssues.explanation: Insurers may offer a large but inadequate lump-sum settlement early in the claim, before the full lifetime cost of a spinal injury is understood. Accepting prematurely closes all future claims.
Insurers may argue that a spinal injury was pre-existing, degenerative, or caused by a prior incident. Pre-injury imaging and expert medical testimony are often needed to counter these claims.
Insurers may require evaluation by a physician of their choosing. IME doctors in catastrophic injury cases frequently minimize the extent and permanence of the injury.

The long-term cost of spinal cord injury — including care, equipment, home modification, and lost earnings — can exceed several million dollars. Insurers typically undervalue these figures significantly.

If the injury occurred at work, workers' comp may not be the only option. If a third party (contractor, equipment manufacturer, property owner) contributed to the injury, a separate civil claim may also be available.

whenToGetLawyer: You or a family member sustained any spinal cord injury, vertebral fracture, or disc herniation in an accident
There is any possibility of partial or complete paralysis
The injury resulted in surgery, hospitalization, or long-term rehabilitation
You are unable to return to your prior occupation or any gainful employment
An insurer is disputing the severity or cause of the spinal injury
You received an early settlement offer before completing your medical treatment
The injury occurred at a workplace, on someone else's property, or involved a commercial vehicle
Long-term or lifetime care costs are anticipated
